does: This paper identifies and addresses a fundamental failure mode in Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), where the implicit reward function learned by the policy tends to overfit and grow in magnitude, leading to degenerate policies that assign zero probability to preferred generations. The authors propose Robust Preference Optimization through Reward Model Distillation, which trains language models to match their implicit rewards (the scaled log-likelihood ratio relative to a reference model) to explicit reward models trained on preference data. To handle reward model uncertainty, they optimize against an ensemble or family of reward models rather than a single reward function, ensuring robustness across plausible reward specifications. The method combines direct policy training with explicit reward model supervision through a distillation objective.
connects: This work builds directly on Direct Preference Optimization and related offline alignment methods that avoid the computational costs of reinforcement learning. It engages with the broader literature on reward modeling in language model alignment, which has long recognized challenges in training accurate reward models from preference data. The paper extends research on implicit reward functions in policy learning and addresses known pathologies in optimization-based alignment where policies can exploit gaps between the learned reward and true preferences. The distillation approach connects to work on using explicit models as targets for implicit objectives, while the ensemble-based robustness reasoning draws from uncertainty quantification in machine learning and distributionally robust optimization.
enables: This work opens directions for more stable and reliable offline alignment of language models by establishing explicit reward distillation as a mechanism for controlling implicit reward behavior. It enables future research on characterizing when and why preference optimization methods overfit, and on developing principled methods to incorporate reward model uncertainty into policy training. The ensemble-based robustness framework suggests further work on scaling robust alignment methods to larger model and preference dataset sizes, and on combining explicit reward models with policy optimization in tighter feedback loops. The approach also motivates investigation into better uncertainty estimates for reward models themselves, and into verification methods that ensure learned policies respect true preference distributions rather than exploiting artifacts of the training procedure.
